text_fields.419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_fd1:
At Microsoft, I built a Resilience Automation Platform that cut operational toil by 30% and freed engineering capacity for higher-impact customer work - essentially the same trade-off Deepgram's Applied Engineering team is trying to make. I also built an AI agent that reduced planning cycle time by 39% and increased drill capacity by 21%, and I developed self-service runbooks that let customers resolve complex issues independently and reduced escalation load on senior engineers. The problems Deepgram is solving - scaling technical support through automation, building tools that let customers self-serve, and keeping engineers focused on high-value engagements - are problems I've been working on directly.

What draws me to Deepgram specifically is that voice AI is a genuinely hard infrastructure problem, not just a wrapper on existing models. The fact that Deepgram has processed over 50,000 years of audio and built voice-native foundation models from the ground up - not retrofitted from text - tells me the technical depth here is real. I want to work at the layer where AI meets production infrastructure at scale, and Deepgram is building exactly that foundation for the next wave of voice applications. The AI-first operating culture also appeals to me - I've already been building AI tooling into my own workflows and want to be somewhere that treats that as a baseline expectation, not a differentiator.
text_fields.419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_fa3:
My core approach is to start with discovery before jumping to solutions. At Microsoft, I conducted discovery with 20+ service teams to quantify exactly 81 hours of manual toil per region launch. That number didn't come from assumptions - it came from structured conversations where I asked teams to walk me through their actual workflows step by step. That data then drove a roadmap targeting the highest-impact automation paths, which ultimately generated $3M in accelerated revenue and cut region launch time by 28%. The specificity of the finding is what made the solution credible and got cross-functional buy-in across 140+ engineering teams with competing priorities.

I've also used customer escalations as a signal, not just a ticket queue. When I saw recurring failure patterns across support escalations at Azure, I collaborated with engineering teams to advocate for roadmap changes that reduced repeat incident volume by 18%. That required translating raw support data into a product argument - showing engineering why a pattern mattered and what fixing it would actually cost versus prevent. Similarly, I ran 11+ user interviews with Azure service teams to surface API workflow gaps, and those findings shipped as intent-based YAML generation features the following quarter. The through line in all of these is treating customer pain as structured data that can drive technical decisions, rather than noise to be managed.
text_fields.419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_a75:
The most durable thing I've built for scale is documentation that actually removes the need to ask me. At Microsoft, I developed technical runbooks and self-service documentation that were adopted across 6 partner teams. The goal wasn't to write comprehensive guides - it was to identify the specific moments where engineers were reaching out to escalate and eliminate those moments with targeted, actionable content. That reduced escalation load on senior engineers and let the broader team handle more complex engagements without bottlenecks.

Beyond documentation, I've leaned heavily on automation to multiply capacity. I built an AI agent to automate drill planning workflows, which cut planning cycle time by 39% and increased drill capacity by 21%. That's a direct example of using tooling to let one person do what previously required more cycles or more people. I also built a Power BI dashboard that tracked operational toil across 140+ teams, which gave leadership a scalable way to prioritize support investments without needing me in every conversation. The pattern across all of these is the same: identify where expertise is the bottleneck, then build something that removes that bottleneck - whether it's a doc, a dashboard, or an automated system - so the team can scale without linearly scaling headcount.
text_fields.419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_988:
The most impactful AI system I've built is an AI agent I designed and deployed at Microsoft to automate drill planning workflows for Azure's resilience testing program. Before this, scheduling and planning each resilience drill was a heavily manual process - coordinating across customer teams, mapping dependencies, validating readiness criteria, and generating documentation. It was a cycle that consumed significant engineering time and capped how many drills we could run.

I built the agent to handle the repeatable, structured portions of that workflow end to end. It ingested inputs about the target environment, dependency graph, and readiness requirements, then generated a complete drill plan - including scheduling recommendations, stakeholder notifications, and pre-drill checklists - without requiring manual assembly at each step. I used LLM-based reasoning for the parts of planning that required interpreting ambiguous inputs or adapting to environment-specific constraints, and layered deterministic logic on top for the outputs that needed to be consistent and auditable.

The measurable outcome was a 39% reduction in planning cycle time and a 21% increase in drill capacity - meaning we could run more drills with the same team, and engineers could redirect the recovered time toward the complex, customer-facing escalations that actually required human judgment. That's the framing I care most about with AI tooling: not automation for its own sake, but freeing high-skill people to do the work only they can do.

radios.name: 419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_8d51f3af-cef9-4ac0-8557-e94a4cf27403
radios.label: Are you located within the United States?

radios.label: Are you located in the San Francisco Bay Area?

radios.label: Will you now or in the future require visa sponsorship (H1B, etc) for employment in the United States?

radios.label: How long has it been since the last time you wrote code for a non-personal (e.g. for your job, an open-source contribution, or a charity/nonprofit) project AND committed it to a repository?

radios.name: 419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_2592f14d-bf4d-435d-9bde-0df553e11975
radios.label: What percentage of your time in your current/last role was spent directly engaging with customers or prospects on technical matters?

radios.name: 419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_cad85db8-c6fa-41d0-b364-05bc977ed25d
radios.label: This role requires client interaction. Have you previously or are you open to working with external clients?

radios.name: 419c196b-6e31-4345-84d3-562a245f008c_683d7609-fdb2-4113-9b9c-90f20052c4af
radios.label: Have you designed or implemented solutions to address recurring technical challenges for customers? If yes, briefly describe an example.

cover_overrides.label: what approaches have you used to translate customer needs into technical solutions?
what tools or methods have you used to scale your technical expertise across multiple customer engagements?
what is the most impressive thing you’ve personally built or automated with ai? describe exactly what you did, how it worked, and the measurable outcome

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment: Resolve dryrun field ids -> actual DOM ids (Ashby sometimes prefixes with form id).

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment:
chain_005 P1 (2026-05-26): native-setter FAST PATH. Tries
`_valueTracker.setValue` + input-event for each plain text
field. Chain_004 verified this DOES propagate React state
for Ashby String/Email/Phone/Number/Url/LongText on Sentry
& OpenAI tenants (5–10x faster than CDP keystrokes).
Driver MUST verify by re-reading each input.value after a
~50ms tick; any field whose read-back != requested value
falls through to the CDP-keystroke step below. Combobox /
Location fields are intentionally NOT in text_fields
(they go through ashby.location_typeahead_fill instead).
Kill switch: set ashby_filler.USE_NATIVE_SETTER_FAST_PATH=False.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce

args.comment:
FIX 1 (Harvey 671): for each (resolved_dom_id, value), driver must
  1. snapshot the input (ref by `#<dom_id>`), 2. act:click on the
  ref to focus, 3. clear via Ctrl+A then Delete keys, 4. act:type the
  value as REAL CDP keystrokes (NOT via JS native value setter).
chain_005 P1 (2026-05-26): driver should SKIP fields that the
fast-path step above already populated successfully. Only fields
whose read-back value != requested need CDP keystrokes.
date_fields (see below) ALSO go through this step — driver should
send an extra Enter key after typing each date_field value to
commit the react-datepicker selection.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment:
chain_028 (Speak 1015 guard): self-contained async JS that
handles Ashby Location typeahead end-to-end. Per field:
  1. setNative + look for [role=option] -> pick best match.
  2. Per-char KeyboardEvent fallback (chain_026 async-
     typeahead recipe), wait up to 2500ms for options.
  3. Free-text + Tab/blur commit as last resort.
ALWAYS returns {resolved: [{fid, method, picked, post_value}],
unresolved: [{fid, reason, required, post_value}]}. Never
throws. Driver: if any required field is unresolved, mark
BLOCKED with structured reason; if only optional fields
unresolved, proceed to submit (Location is often optional).
Kill switch: ashby_filler.USE_LOCATION_TYPEAHEAD_SELF_CONTAINED=False
reverts to the legacy two-step manual recipe.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment:
FIX 2 (Harvey 671): resolve each radio group -> matching <label>
element id + bounding-rect center. Returns {picked: [{label_id, cx, cy, label_text, kind}]}.
chain_033 (2026-05-30 Notion fix): `kind` is either `radio_label`
(traditional <input type=radio>) or `yesno_button` (Notion / Speak /
Baseten / Plain style — <div class=_yesno_>Yes/No buttons + hidden
checkbox). Driver behavior is IDENTICAL for both kinds: scrollIntoView
on the element + `act:clickCoords` at (cx, cy). Do NOT use JS .click()
on the <input> — Ashby React validator ignores synthetic clicks.
Post-click verify (chain_033): re-evaluate JS_VERIFY —
`yesno_buttons_active` should equal `yesno_groups` (each Boolean field
now has an active button). If not, retry the missing entry's clickCoords.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment:
FIX 3 (Harvey 671): driver must NOT use selector='#_systemfield_resume'
(silently no-ops on multi-file-input pages). Instead:
  1. browser.snapshot to get the page.
  2. Find the 'Upload File' button whose nearest ancestor label/
     section text contains 'Resume' (and NOT 'Transcript' or
     'Cover Letter' or other file-field labels).
  3. browser.upload ref=<that_button_ref> paths=[...].
  4. Verify by re-snapshot: page should show the resume filename.
FALLBACK (single file input on page): browser.upload
  element='#_systemfield_resume' paths=[...]. MUST use `element=`,
  NOT `selector=` — selector= silently no-ops with files=0.
  See _upload-regression-diag-20260525.md (2026-05-25).
POST-UPLOAD VERIFY (required): evaluate
  () => document.querySelector('#_systemfield_resume')?.files?.length || 0
  If 0, treat as UploadFailedError and retry once with element= form.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment: Verify form state: counts of filled inputs, picked radios/checkboxes, attached files, submit button presence.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment:
FIX 5 (strict-Ashby reCAPTCHA v3): driver should
  1. evaluate `() => Array.from(document.scripts).map(s=>s.src).filter(s=>s.includes('recaptcha'))`
     to confirm the v3 loader + extract sitekey from `?render=...`.
  2. If sitekey == known_strict_sitekey (Ashby shared infra), call
     captcha_solver.CaptchaSolver(vendor='capsolver').solve_recaptcha_v3(
       sitekey, page_url, page_action='submit', min_score=0.7)
  3. On token: evaluate to inject —
     `(t) => { const ids=['g-recaptcha-response','g-recaptcha-response-100000'];
      for (const id of ids) { let el=document.getElementById(id); if (!el){
        el=document.createElement('textarea'); el.id=id; el.name=id; el.style.display='none';
        document.body.appendChild(el);} el.value=t;} return {injected: ids.length};}`
  4. THEN click Submit. If SolverNotConfigured — skip (log
     `captcha-skipped-no-key`); submit will likely spam-flag but
     attempt is still required per per-role doctrine.
  5. If post-submit DOM still shows `[role=alert]` containing
     `flagged as possible spam`, the v3 score was sub-threshold;
     log per-tenant + bail (no infinite retry loop).
NEW 2026-05-27: easiest path is to just call
  `from captcha_presubmit import solve_and_inject_recaptcha_v3;
   solve_and_inject_recaptcha_v3(frame, **driver_exec.kwargs)`
  which handles detect+solve+inject in one call. No-op when
  ENABLE_CAPSOLVER!=1 — safe to leave wired permanently.

args.label: deepgram-be9609b4-b48d-4421-90e5-69049ad0d6ce
args.comment: FINAL: click 'Submit Application'. Run only after VERIFY passes manual review.
